ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean Yong Tau Foo; 'stuffed tofu food product' is the closest defensible match. Exact FoodOn URI for this composite dish is not available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — mixed dishes with fish/meat and vegetables in broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yong Tau Foo. A0CKW (composite mixed dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Yong Tau Foo as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Yong Tau Foo in clear soup consists of 7 assorted stuffed pieces served in a bowl of clear broth, weighing approximately 350 g including broth.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (clear soup) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, 7 pieces, mixed), cr_id F08010063. All per-100g macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Tofu and Fish Paste Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-GI tofu and fish paste composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with tofu-based and fish paste composite dishes with low carbohydrate content (3.21 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 11.2) / 100 ≈ 5.0 for 350 g serving; however, per the input record's embedded GI database value of GL 1.4 per 100g, the per-serving GL at 350g is approximately 2.5 after rounding to a standard serving reference. GI confidence is moderate (0.43) given the analogous nature of the estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 368 mg per 100 g; broth and dipping sauce contribute additional sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium broth, and avoid or minimise the soy-based dipping sauce, as the combined sodium from broth and sauce can exceed recommended single-meal limits of 600–800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.21 g/100g), low sugar (1.13 g/100g), low GI (~45), and moderate protein (3.64 g/100g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo in clear soup without rice or noodles is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content, low GI, and moderate protein; sweet dipping sauces and carbohydrate-based accompaniments should be avoided to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 368 mg/100g is borderline for cardiovascular risk; fat is moderate at 2.4 g/100g with low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and moderate total fat content are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the elevated sodium from broth and dipping sauce warrants caution; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium broth and avoid the dipping sauce to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, chili) may trigger solanine-related inflammation in susceptible individuals; sodium 368 mg/100g approaches RA dietary threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should request substitution of eggplant and chili pieces, and should minimise the soy-based dipping sauce to reduce sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, 7 pieces, mixed) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish of Hakka Chinese origin consisting of seven assorted items — typically including stuffed tofu, bitter gourd, eggplant, okra, chili, fish balls, and fried tofu puffs — filled with a seasoned fish paste and served in a light, clear broth. The dish is boiled or lightly fried before being placed in the broth. The texture is soft and yielding with a mild, savoury flavour from the fish paste and broth. It is a customisable dish where diners select their preferred combination of items. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (49 kcal/100g), low in carbohydrates (3.21 g/100g), moderate in protein (3.64 g/100g), and moderate in fat (2.4 g/100g), making it one of the lighter hawker options. Sodium is elevated at 368 mg/100g due to the broth and fish paste, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low GI (~45) and low sugar content (1.13 g/100g) make it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without rice or noodles. The dish is dairy-free and can be halal-certified depending on the stall. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or light meal in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010063) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate with moderate confidence (0.43) as no direct measurement exists.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100g based on typical fish paste and tofu composition; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB record and is an approximation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient data, and moisture content for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (F08010063) or available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to tofu and fish paste composite dishes with low carbohydrate content; no direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup) was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated at approximately 2.5 for a 350 g serving. The 'vegetarian' dietary tag from the input has been removed as the dish contains fish paste. The 'keto' dietary tag has been retained as a cultural/community tag only; clinical keto suitability should be verified by a dietitian. Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, chili) are common inclusions but the exact mix varies by stall and customer selection.
